title: CDMG
lean_code_with_comments:
structure CDMG (Node : Type*) [DecidableEq Node] where
  J : Finset Node
  V : Finset Node
  hJV_disj : Disjoint J V
  E : Finset (Node × Node)
  hE_subset : ∀ ⦃e : Node × Node⦄, e ∈ E → e.1 ∈ J ∪ V ∧ e.2 ∈ V
  L : Finset (Node × Node)
  hL_subset : ∀ ⦃e : Node × Node⦄, e ∈ L → e.1 ∈ V ∧ e.2 ∈ V
  hL_irrefl : ∀ ⦃v1 v2 : Node⦄, (v1, v2) ∈ L → v1 ≠ v2
  hL_symm : ∀ ⦃v1 v2 : Node⦄, (v1, v2) ∈ L → (v2, v1) ∈ L
lean_code_without_comments:
structure CDMG (Node : Type*) [DecidableEq Node] where
  J : Finset Node
  V : Finset Node
  hJV_disj : Disjoint J V
  E : Finset (Node × Node)
  hE_subset : ∀ ⦃e : Node × Node⦄, e ∈ E → e.1 ∈ J ∪ V ∧ e.2 ∈ V
  L : Finset (Node × Node)
  hL_subset : ∀ ⦃e : Node × Node⦄, e ∈ L → e.1 ∈ V ∧ e.2 ∈ V
  hL_irrefl : ∀ ⦃v1 v2 : Node⦄, (v1, v2) ∈ L → v1 ≠ v2
  hL_symm : ∀ ⦃v1 v2 : Node⦄, (v1, v2) ∈ L → (v2, v1) ∈ L
lean_explanation:
The structure `CDMG Node` is parameterised by an ambient vertex type `Node : Type*` together with the typeclass `[DecidableEq Node]`. The variable `Node` plays the role of the lecture notes' silent ambient carrier of vertices; `DecidableEq Node` is the minimal typeclass needed so that `Finset` can carry the vertex and edge sets and decide membership in the kernel.

The structure bundles four data fields and five constraint fields. The data are `J : Finset Node` (input nodes), `V : Finset Node` (output nodes), `E : Finset (Node × Node)` (directed edges), and `L : Finset (Node × Node)` (bidirected edges). All four are wrapped as `Finset`s, which packages finiteness into the carrier itself rather than as an external `Set.Finite` or `Fintype` witness.

The constraint fields encode the side conditions. `hJV_disj : Disjoint J V` invokes Mathlib's lattice-theoretic `Disjoint` predicate, which on `Finset` is equivalent to $J \cap V = \emptyset$. The subset constraints `hE_subset` and `hL_subset` are universally quantified over an edge `e : Node × Node` and read off its projections `e.1` and `e.2` to assert the LN-side $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ and $L \subseteq V \times V$. The bidirected channel additionally carries `hL_irrefl`, asserting $(v_1, v_2) \in L \Rightarrow v_1 \neq v_2$, and `hL_symm`, asserting $(v_1, v_2) \in L \Rightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$.

One Mathlib-Lean idiom worth flagging: the constraint fields use the strict-implicit binders `⦃e : Node × Node⦄` and `⦃v1 v2 : Node⦄`. These double-bracket binders behave like ordinary implicit arguments at unification time but defer elaboration until the next explicit argument is supplied, which prevents `simp` and `apply` from trying to discharge the constraint hypothesis on every term of the right shape. The notation `J ∪ V` is the `Finset` union, and `e.1`, `e.2` are the first and second projections of an ordered pair.
design_choices:
**A bespoke `structure`, rather than a typeclass or a reuse of Mathlib's graph theory.** A CDMG is data — there is no canonical CDMG to be inferred on a given vertex type — so `class` and `abbrev` were rejected. Mathlib's `SimpleGraph` is undirected and single-sort, with no bidirected channel; `Quiver` carries ordered edges only, with neither an input/output partition nor a symmetric sub-relation. A custom record is the cheapest encoding that holds all four pieces (J/V partition, directed channel `E`, bidirected channel `L`) and lets every downstream definition destructure uniformly as `G.J`, `G.V`, `G.E`, `G.L`.

**A single-sort vertex type `Node`, not two separate types for inputs and outputs.** Inputs and outputs in the LN form a single ambient set over which downstream constructions (parents, ancestors, siblings, topological orders) range freely; encoding them as two distinct types would force a `Sum` coproduct and coercions at every use site. The disjointness $J \cap V = \emptyset$ is reinstated as an explicit structure field, which is also load-bearing because hard intervention later moves vertices between `J` and `V`.

**`Finset` carriers throughout, not `Set` with a finiteness witness.** `Set Node` paired with `Set.Finite` or a bundled `Fintype` was rejected on two grounds. First, the finiteness witness would have to thread through every consumer as a second field, whereas `Finset` bakes it into the carrier. Second, `Finset` carries decidable membership, which the family-set definitions (parents, children, ancestors, descendants, siblings) need for `Finset.filter`, `Finset.image`, and kernel-level comparisons.

**Bidirected edges as ordered pairs with an explicit symmetry constraint, not as `Sym2`.** The lecture notes describe `L` as a subset of the quotient $(V \times V)/{\sim}$ under the swap relation. Mathlib's `Sym2` would realise this directly, but at the cost of a `Sym2.mk` / `Sym2.lift` dance at every downstream destructuring site — sibling sets, bidirected walks, intervention-time edge rewriting. The ordered-pair encoding lets `L` share every `Finset (Node × Node)` lemma with `E`. The explicit cost is that each undirected bidirected edge appears twice in `L`, so downstream code that counts or iterates over bidirected edges must either divide by two or pick a canonical orientation.

**Irreflexivity and symmetry are separate fields, not a single conjunction.** Some downstream definitions need only irreflexivity (ruling out trivial bidirected loops) and others need only symmetry (reversing direction along bidirected walks); bundling them would force each such call site to unpack a conjunction.

**No type-level mutual exclusion between `E` and `L`, and the empty CDMG is admitted.** The lecture notes' "disjoint" qualifier on the edge sets is read as type-level only: the same ordered pair $(v, w)$ may belong to both `E` and `L`. Likewise, the empty graph ($J = V = \emptyset$) is a legal inhabitant, mirroring the literal LN; rows that need non-emptiness or stricter edge-set disjointness add the constraint at their use sites.
